title: Convergence Foundations & SIP Signaling
summary: How embassy voice migrated from circuit-switched TDM PBX onto a single converged IP network, and how SIP establishes, modifies, and tears down calls.
content:
Convergence means running voice, data, and video over one shared IP infrastructure instead of maintaining separate networks. The legacy model was a TDM (Time-Division Multiplexing) PBX that switched 64 kbps voice circuits over dedicated copper, with analog/digital phones cabled to line cards and trunks to the public telephone network. An IP-PBX (call server / call manager) replaces those dedicated circuits with packetized voice carried over the embassy LAN/WAN, so a single Ethernet drop and switch port serves both the workstation and the IP phone.

The payoff is consolidated cabling, centralized administration across posts, remote/teleworker softphones, and integration with unified communications (presence, chat, video, voicemail-to-email). The trade-off is that voice becomes just another application competing for bandwidth, so the network must now guarantee quality and security that the old TDM circuit provided inherently.

• TDM PBX: deterministic, dedicated 64 kbps channels, very reliable, but siloed, costly to scale, hard to integrate.
• IP-PBX: flexible, software-driven, UC-ready, but exposed to congestion, latency, and IP-based attack surface.

SIP (Session Initiation Protocol, RFC 3261) is the dominant signaling protocol that sets up, modifies, and ends sessions. SIP is text-based and request/response oriented like HTTP, using SIP URIs (sip:user@domain). It only negotiates the session; the actual audio rides a separate media protocol (RTP). Session parameters such as codecs and media ports are described inside the SIP body using SDP (Session Description Protocol).

Key SIP methods: INVITE initiates a call (carrying SDP offer); ACK confirms the final response to an INVITE, completing the three-way handshake; BYE terminates an established call; REGISTER binds a user's SIP address to their current IP/contact at the registrar; plus CANCEL (abort a pending INVITE), OPTIONS (query capabilities), and REFER (transfer).

SIP response codes mirror HTTP families: 1xx provisional (180 Ringing, 100 Trying); 2xx success (200 OK); 3xx redirection (302 Moved Temporarily); 4xx client error (404 Not Found, 486 Busy Here, 401/407 auth required); 5xx server error (503 Service Unavailable); 6xx global failure (603 Decline).

SIP server roles: a registrar accepts REGISTER and stores location bindings; a proxy server routes/forwards SIP requests toward their destination (stateful or stateless) without owning the media; a redirect server returns a 3xx telling the client where to send the request instead of forwarding it. A B2BUA (Back-to-Back User Agent) terminates one call leg and originates another, sitting in the middle of both signaling and call control — IP-PBXs and session border controllers act as B2BUAs to anchor media, hide topology, and bridge features.

SIP trunks replace physical PSTN trunk lines with an IP connection to an ITSP (Internet Telephony Service Provider), delivering DID numbers and outbound calling over the WAN. For security, SIP signaling should run over TLS (sips: URIs, typically TCP/5061) to encrypt and authenticate the signaling channel, preventing eavesdropping on call metadata and registration hijacking — paired with SRTP to protect the media.
keyPoints: Convergence = voice + data (+ video) on one IP network; IP-PBX replaces dedicated TDM 64 kbps circuits.
TDM PBX gives deterministic dedicated channels; IP-PBX gives flexibility/UC but must engineer QoS and security.
SIP (RFC 3261) is text-based signaling only; it negotiates sessions while RTP carries the actual media.
SDP inside SIP messages describes codecs, media IP/ports, and other session parameters.
Core SIP methods: INVITE, ACK, BYE, REGISTER, plus CANCEL, OPTIONS, REFER.
Response codes: 180 Ringing, 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 486 Busy Here; families 1xx-6xx like HTTP.
Server roles: registrar (location bindings), proxy (forwards requests), redirect (returns 3xx).
B2BUA terminates and re-originates both legs, anchoring media and hiding topology (PBX/SBC behavior).
SIP trunks replace physical PSTN trunks with an IP link to an ITSP carrying DID numbers.
Secure signaling with TLS (sips:, TCP/5061); pair with SRTP for full call confidentiality.

flashcards.front: Define network convergence in VoIP terms.
flashcards.back: Carrying voice, data, and video over a single shared IP network instead of separate dedicated networks.

flashcards.front: TDM PBX vs IP-PBX — core difference?
flashcards.back: TDM PBX switches dedicated 64 kbps circuits over copper; IP-PBX packetizes voice over the IP LAN/WAN via a software call server.

flashcards.front: What does SIP actually do, and what does it NOT do?
flashcards.back: SIP sets up, modifies, and tears down sessions (signaling). It does NOT carry the audio — RTP does.

flashcards.front: What protocol inside SIP describes codecs and media ports?
flashcards.back: SDP (Session Description Protocol), carried in the SIP message body.
Initiates a call/session, carrying an SDP offer of media capabilities.
Acknowledges the final response to an INVITE, completing the three-way handshake.
Terminates an established session (hangs up the call).
Binds a user's SIP address to their current IP/contact location at the registrar.
Ringing — provisional (1xx) response, the remote endpoint is alerting.
OK — success (2xx), the request (e.g., INVITE) was accepted.
Busy Here — the called party is busy (4xx client error).
Not Found — the requested user/URI does not exist (4xx client error).

flashcards.front: Proxy vs registrar vs redirect server?
flashcards.back: Proxy forwards/routes SIP requests; registrar stores user location bindings (REGISTER); redirect server returns a 3xx telling the client where to send the request.
Back-to-Back User Agent — terminates one call leg and originates another, controlling both signaling and media; used by IP-PBXs and SBCs.
An IP-based connection to an ITSP that replaces physical PSTN trunk lines, carrying DID numbers and PSTN calling over the WAN.
With TLS (sips: URIs, typically TCP/5061) to encrypt/authenticate signaling; paired with SRTP for media.

title: Media Transport, Codecs & Voice Quality
summary: How RTP carries digitized voice, how codecs and impairments shape quality, and the QoS engineering needed to make converged voice reliable at an embassy.
content:
Once SIP negotiates a session, the audio itself travels as RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol, usually over UDP). RTP packets carry sequence numbers and timestamps so the receiver can reorder, detect loss, and play out audio with correct timing. RTCP (RTP Control Protocol) runs alongside RTP and carries quality statistics — jitter, packet loss, round-trip delay — used for monitoring and adaptation. SRTP (Secure RTP) adds encryption and authentication to the media stream so conversations cannot be intercepted; keys are typically exchanged via SDP/DTLS-SRTP and the whole call is protected when SRTP is paired with TLS signaling.

Codecs convert analog voice to digital and back, trading bandwidth for quality. G.711 (PCM) is uncompressed toll-quality narrowband at ~64 kbps — the baseline, low CPU, high bandwidth. G.729 compresses to ~8 kbps for bandwidth-constrained WAN links at some quality cost. G.722 is wideband (HD voice, ~7 kHz audio) at ~64 kbps for noticeably clearer calls. Opus is a modern, royalty-free, variable-bitrate codec that scales from narrowband to fullband and adapts to network conditions. Narrowband samples ~300–3400 Hz (legacy phone quality); wideband roughly doubles that range for natural-sounding HD voice — both endpoints must support the same codec.

• DTMF (touch-tone digits) should not be sent as audio through low-bitrate codecs that distort it; instead use RFC 2833 / RFC 4733 telephone-event packets (named-telephone-events in RTP) or SIP INFO so IVRs reliably detect keypresses.
• VAD (Voice Activity Detection) with silence suppression stops sending packets during silence to save bandwidth; comfort noise generation (CNG) then synthesizes low-level background noise at the far end so the line doesn't sound dead.
• PLC (Packet Loss Concealment) masks lost packets by interpolating or repeating audio so brief loss is less audible.
• Echo arises two ways: acoustic echo (far-end speaker bleeding back into the microphone, common on speakerphones) and hybrid echo (impedance mismatch at the analog 2-wire/4-wire conversion in PSTN gateways); echo cancellers address both.

Voice quality depends on three impairments. Latency (one-way mouth-to-ear delay) should stay under ~150 ms per ITU-T G.114 for natural conversation; 150–400 ms is degraded; beyond ~400 ms is generally unacceptable. Jitter is the variation in packet inter-arrival time; a jitter buffer (de-jitter buffer) holds packets briefly to smooth playout, trading a little added delay for steadiness — too small drops late packets, too large adds latency. Packet loss above a few percent causes audible gaps even with PLC. MOS (Mean Opinion Score, 1–5) summarizes perceived quality; ~4.0+ is good toll quality, and tools estimate MOS from these metrics.

To protect voice, the network applies QoS. Traffic is classified and marked with DSCP in the IP header — voice media uses EF (Expedited Forwarding, DSCP 46) for strict-priority/low-latency queuing, while call signaling uses a class like CS3/AF31. Switches and routers then place EF traffic in a priority queue so it isn't delayed behind bulk data. CAC (Call Admission Control) limits how many concurrent calls a link will accept, rejecting new calls rather than degrading all active ones when bandwidth is exhausted. A dedicated voice VLAN separates phone traffic from data at Layer 2, simplifying QoS trust boundaries, security, and addressing — IP phones are commonly auto-provisioned into the voice VLAN (e.g., via LLDP-MED/CDP) while the attached PC stays on the data VLAN.
keyPoints: RTP (over UDP) carries the audio with sequence numbers and timestamps; RTCP reports jitter, loss, and delay.
SRTP encrypts/authenticates the media; combine with TLS signaling for end-to-end confidentiality.
G.711 = ~64 kbps toll-quality narrowband; G.729 = ~8 kbps compressed; G.722 = wideband HD; Opus = adaptive VBR.
Narrowband ~300-3400 Hz (legacy) vs wideband (HD) doubles the audio range; both ends must share a codec.
Send DTMF as RFC 2833/4733 telephone-events or SIP INFO, not as compressed audio.
VAD/silence suppression saves bandwidth; comfort noise (CNG) keeps the line from sounding dead.
PLC conceals lost packets; echo is acoustic (mic/speaker) or hybrid (2-to-4-wire mismatch at gateways).
Latency target ~150 ms one-way (ITU-T G.114); jitter buffer smooths arrival variance at the cost of delay.
MOS (1-5) rates perceived quality; ~4.0+ is good toll quality.
QoS: mark voice EF/DSCP 46 (signaling CS3/AF31), use priority queuing, CAC, and a separate voice VLAN.

flashcards.back: Carries real-time media (audio/video) over UDP, with sequence numbers and timestamps for reordering and timing.
Travels alongside RTP carrying quality statistics — jitter, packet loss, round-trip delay — for monitoring/adaptation.
Secure RTP — adds encryption and authentication to the media stream to prevent eavesdropping.
~64 kbps uncompressed PCM, toll-quality narrowband; low CPU but high bandwidth (the baseline).
Compresses voice to ~8 kbps to save bandwidth on constrained WAN links, at some quality cost.
Wideband (HD voice, ~7 kHz audio) at ~64 kbps for clearer, more natural calls.
Modern royalty-free variable-bitrate codec that scales narrowband to fullband and adapts to network conditions.
Narrowband ~300-3400 Hz (legacy phone); wideband roughly doubles the range for HD voice.

flashcards.front: How should DTMF digits be transmitted in VoIP?
flashcards.back: As RFC 2833/4733 telephone-event RTP packets or SIP INFO — not as compressed audio that codecs distort.
VAD stops sending packets during silence to save bandwidth; comfort noise (CNG) synthesizes faint background noise so the line isn't dead-silent.
Packet Loss Concealment — masks lost packets by interpolating/repeating audio so brief loss is less audible.
Acoustic = speaker audio bleeding into the mic (speakerphones); hybrid = impedance mismatch at the 2-wire/4-wire conversion in PSTN gateways.

flashcards.front: One-way latency budget for good voice?
flashcards.back: Under ~150 ms mouth-to-ear (ITU-T G.114); 150-400 ms degraded; >400 ms unacceptable.

flashcards.front: What is jitter and what fixes it?
flashcards.back: Jitter is variation in packet inter-arrival time; a jitter buffer holds packets briefly to smooth playout, adding slight delay.
Mean Opinion Score (1-5) rating perceived call quality; ~4.0+ is good toll quality.

flashcards.front: How is voice prioritized with QoS markings?
flashcards.back: Mark media EF (DSCP 46) for strict-priority/low-latency queuing; signaling uses CS3/AF31.
Limits concurrent calls on a link, rejecting new calls rather than degrading all active ones when bandwidth runs out.
Isolates phone traffic from data at Layer 2 for cleaner QoS, security, and addressing; phones auto-join via LLDP-MED/CDP.

title: Gateways, NAT Traversal & Telephony Features/Operations
summary: The infrastructure that bridges VoIP to the PSTN, how calls cross NAT/firewalls securely, and the dial-plan, feature, and operational essentials of running embassy unified communications.
content:
Even a fully converged embassy must still reach the outside telephone world. A media gateway (voice/PSTN gateway) converts between IP packets (RTP) and traditional telephony — analog lines, digital trunks, or TDM circuits — translating signaling and media so an IP-PBX can place calls to the PSTN. Analog interfaces come in two flavors that are frequently confused: FXS (Foreign eXchange Station) ports supply dial tone, ring voltage, and battery to a connected endpoint (a regular analog phone, fax machine, or door phone), while FXO (Foreign eXchange Office) ports connect TO the phone company / a PBX line, receiving dial tone — i.e., FXS feeds a device, FXO faces the office/provider.

For higher capacity, digital trunks use PRI (Primary Rate Interface, ISDN). A T1 PRI (North America) provides 23 bearer (B) voice channels plus 1 signaling (D) channel — '23B+D' on 24 timeslots — while an E1 PRI (Europe/most of the world) provides 30 B channels + 1 D + 1 framing channel across 32 timeslots. The D channel carries call-control signaling for all the bearer channels.

A Session Border Controller (SBC) sits at the edge between the embassy network and the SIP trunk/ITSP. Acting as a B2BUA, it provides security (topology hiding, encryption enforcement, DoS protection, access control), interoperability (SIP normalization/transcoding between dialects and codecs), and NAT/firewall traversal for signaling and media. NAT breaks VoIP because the private IP/ports advertised inside SIP/SDP are invalid on the public side; the ICE framework solves this using STUN (a server tells a client its public mapped address for direct/hairpin connectivity) and TURN (a relay server forwards media when direct paths fail, e.g., symmetric NAT). ICE gathers and tests candidate paths and picks the best working one; SBCs often handle this on behalf of internal phones.

Numbering and routing: E.164 is the international standard format (+ country code + national number, up to 15 digits) ensuring globally unique reachability — essential across multiple posts. The dial plan defines how dialed digits are interpreted and routed, including digit manipulation (stripping/prepending digits, translating extensions to E.164, adding access codes). A DID (Direct Inward Dialing) number routes an external call straight to a specific extension without an operator.

Features the test expects: hunt groups / ring groups distribute incoming calls across a set of extensions (sequential 'hunt' or simultaneous 'ring all'); an IVR / auto-attendant answers and routes callers via menus ('press 1 for...'); MWI (Message Waiting Indicator) lights a lamp / sends a notification when voicemail is waiting; music on hold plays audio to held callers; and T.38 is the standard for reliable fax over IP, carrying fax as data rather than letting compressed codecs corrupt the tones.

Operations and compliance: E911 requires a dispatchable location (street address plus floor/room) be associated with each phone so emergency responders find the caller — critical because IP phones are portable and may move between drops (RAY BAUM'S Act / Kari's Law drive this in the U.S.). Toll fraud prevention guards against attackers hijacking the system to place expensive international calls — via strong SIP/registration credentials, class-of-service/calling restrictions, disabling unused trunks, rate limiting, and monitoring. CDRs (Call Detail Records) log each call's number, time, duration, and disposition, supporting billing, capacity planning, troubleshooting, and security/fraud auditing.
keyPoints: A media/PSTN gateway converts between IP (RTP) voice and analog/digital TDM telephony for PSTN reachability.
FXS supplies dial tone/ring/battery to a device (phone/fax); FXO faces the phone company/PBX and receives dial tone.
T1 PRI = 23B+D (24 timeslots); E1 PRI = 30B + D + framing (32 timeslots); the D channel carries signaling.
An SBC at the network edge gives security (topology hiding), SIP/codec interoperability, and NAT traversal as a B2BUA.
NAT traversal uses ICE with STUN (discover public address) and TURN (relay media when direct paths fail).
E.164 is the international numbering format (+CC + number, max 15 digits) for globally unique reachability.
Dial plan + digit manipulation translate extensions, strip/prepend digits; DID routes external calls to a specific extension.
Hunt/ring groups distribute calls; IVR/auto-attendant routes via menus; MWI signals waiting voicemail.
T.38 carries fax reliably over IP as data; music on hold serves held callers.
E911 requires a dispatchable location per phone; prevent toll fraud; CDRs log calls for billing/troubleshooting/fraud audit.

flashcards.front: What does a media/PSTN gateway do?
flashcards.back: Converts between IP packet voice (RTP) and traditional analog/digital TDM telephony, translating signaling and media to reach the PSTN.

flashcards.front: FXS vs FXO — which is which?
flashcards.back: FXS supplies dial tone/ring/battery to an endpoint (analog phone/fax); FXO connects toward the phone company/PBX and receives dial tone.

flashcards.front: What does a T1 PRI provide?
flashcards.back: 23 bearer (B) voice channels + 1 D signaling channel ('23B+D') across 24 timeslots (North America).

flashcards.front: What does an E1 PRI provide?
flashcards.back: 30 B voice channels + 1 D signaling + 1 framing channel across 32 timeslots (Europe/most of world).

flashcards.front: What is the D channel in PRI?
flashcards.back: The dedicated signaling/call-control channel serving all the bearer (B) channels.

flashcards.front: What is a Session Border Controller (SBC)?
flashcards.back: An edge B2BUA between the network and SIP trunk providing security (topology hiding/DoS), SIP/codec interoperability, and NAT traversal.
Private IP/ports advertised inside SIP/SDP are invalid on the public side, so media/signaling can't reach the endpoint.
STUN tells a client its public mapped address for direct connectivity; TURN relays media when direct paths fail (e.g., symmetric NAT).
Interactive Connectivity Establishment — gathers candidate addresses (using STUN/TURN), tests them, and picks the best working media path.
The international telephone numbering standard: + country code + national number, up to 15 digits, for globally unique reachability.

flashcards.front: What is a dial plan / digit manipulation?
flashcards.back: Rules for interpreting and routing dialed digits — stripping/prepending digits, translating extensions to E.164, adding access codes.
Direct Inward Dialing — an external number that routes straight to a specific extension without an operator.
Both distribute inbound calls across extensions; hunt rings them sequentially, ring-all rings them simultaneously.
An automated system that answers calls and routes them via voice menus ('press 1 for...').
Message Waiting Indicator — lamp/notification telling a user voicemail is waiting.
The standard for reliable fax over IP, carrying fax as data so codecs don't corrupt the tones.
A street address plus floor/room tied to each phone so emergency responders can locate the caller, even as IP phones move.

flashcards.front: What is toll fraud and how is it prevented?
flashcards.back: Attackers hijacking the PBX for costly international calls; prevented via strong credentials, calling restrictions/class-of-service, disabling unused trunks, rate limiting, and monitoring.
Call Detail Records logging number, time, duration, and disposition of each call for billing, capacity planning, troubleshooting, and fraud auditing.
